title: Pricing principles
text: Exposure measures amount risk observed portfolio, typically time coverage. practice, exposure equals policy-years: one year –> exposure = 1 six months –> exposure = 0.5 motor insurance, often expressed vehicle-years. exposure –> credible observations less exposure –> volatile outcomes Key principle Pricing metrics expressed per unit exposure.

title: Pricing principles
text: Insurance losses typically decomposed two components: frequency: number claims per unit exposure severity: average claim size , risk premium derived: risk premium = expected loss per unit exposure can written : risk premium = frequency × severity equivalently: risk premium = total loss / exposure risk premium also referred : pure premium burning cost represents expected cost claims, excluding expenses margins.

title: Add expert-based relativities to a refinement workflow — add_relativities
text: model_variable variable already used GLM. split_variable detailed variable portfolio data used split one levels model_variable. relativities argument named list describing splits, usually built relativities() split_level(). step stored rating_refinement object applied refit() called. normalize = TRUE, supplied relativities normalised using exposure refined split keeps original level effect average. helps prevent expert split unintentionally changing total premium level original model group. use add_relativities() intended refinement within already reasonably homogeneous GLM segment. redistributes existing coefficient across sublevels using exposure-weighted relativities, preserving overall level original coefficient. useful mild heterogeneity, commercial refinement, monotonic tariff differentiation, expert-based segmentation within stable risk group original GLM coefficient broadly representative. Limitations method substitute creating separate risk segment original GLM coefficient distorted. example, suppose broad industry segment contains many relatively stable businesses, chemical companies drive losses representing little exposure. fitted industry coefficient may dominated chemical companies' experience. Applying exposure-weighted relativities inside segment may barely reduce coefficient large exposure group, original coefficient already pulled upward outlier subgroup. situation often better create separate GLM factor level, derive separate tariff segment, apply explicit segmentation acceptation rules, instead relying add_relativities().

title: Add expert-based relativities to a refinement workflow — add_relativities
code: portfolio <- data.frame(   claims = c(1, 2, 1, 3, 2, 4),   exposure = rep(1, 6),   construction = factor(c("residential", "commercial", "residential",                           "commercial", "residential", "commercial")),   construction_detail = factor(c("flat", "shop", "house",                                  "office", "flat", "shop")) )  model <- glm(   claims ~ construction + offset(log(exposure)),   family = poisson(),   data = portfolio )  relativities <- relativities(   split_level(     "residential",     new_levels = c("flat", "house"),     relativities = c(0.95, 1.05)   ) )  refined <- prepare_refinement(model, data = portfolio) |>   add_relativities(     model_variable = "construction",     split_variable = "construction_detail",     relativities = relativities,     exposure = "exposure"   )

title: Add smoothing to a refinement workflow — add_smoothing
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ library(dplyr)  age_policyholder_frequency <- risk_factor_gam(   data = MTPL,   claim_count = "nclaims",   risk_factor = "age_policyholder",   exposure = "exposure" )  age_segments_freq <- derive_tariff_segments(age_policyholder_frequency)  dat <- MTPL |>   add_tariff_segments(age_segments_freq, name = "age_policyholder_freq_cat") |>   mutate(across(where(is.character), as.factor)) |>   mutate(across(where(is.factor), ~ set_reference_level(., exposure)))  freq <- glm(   nclaims ~ bm + age_policyholder_freq_cat,   offset = log(exposure),   family = poisson(),   data = dat )  sev <- glm(   amount ~ zip,   weights = nclaims,   family = Gamma(link = "log"),   data = dat |> filter(amount > 0) )  premium_df <- dat |>   add_prediction(freq, sev) |>   mutate(premium = pred_nclaims_freq * pred_amount_sev)  burn_unrestricted <- glm(   premium ~ zip + bm + age_policyholder_freq_cat,   weights = exposure,   family = Gamma(link = "log"),   data = premium_df )  ref <- prepare_refinement(burn_unrestricted) |>   add_smoothing(     model_variable = "age_policyholder_freq_cat",     source_variable = "age_policyholder",     breaks = seq(18, 95, 5),     weights = "exposure"   ) } # }

what: Calculate an excess-loss vector for capped severity modelling — calculate_excess_loss
title: Calculate an excess-loss vector for capped severity modelling — calculate_excess_loss
text: Estimate allocate expected cost large claims chosen cap. pricing work, severity model often fitted capped claim amounts, example pmin(claim_amount, 100000), large claims can sparse volatile model directly regular severity GLM. calculate_excess_loss() helps add missing part back estimating claim cost cap allocating amount selected portfolio rows. resulting vector can added technical risk premium pure premium. excess-loss amount part technical risk premium. meant commercial loading margin. add_excess_loss() can used afterwards copy calculated vectors data frame without recalculating . method determines total excess-loss amount: "empirical" Uses observed excess excess_threshold. transparent easy reconcile, sensitive large claims. "bootstrap" Bootstraps claims fit_threshold calculates excess excess_threshold sample. keeps method data-driven giving sense sampling variability sparse large-loss experience. bootstrap_smooth = TRUE, sampled large claims perturbed around observed values using bootstrap_bandwidth. avoids relying exact historical claim amounts can useful large-loss experience sparse, discrete strongly influenced individual claims. "manual" Uses manual_excess total excess-loss amount. useful amount comes expert judgement, governance, reinsurance information external benchmark. allocation_method determines total excess-loss amount allocated back rows: "exposure" Allocates proportion allocation_weights, allocation factor 1 selected rows. practice means every selected row receives excess-loss loading per unit allocation weight, example per unit earned exposure. stable choice large-loss experience sparse support credible split groups. "historical_excess" Derives allocation factors observed excess excess_threshold per allocation group. Groups produced observed excess receive larger allocation factor, allowing allocation weight. risk-sensitive exposure allocation, can volatile large claims dominate experience. "bootstrap_excess" Derives allocation factors bootstrapped excess shares per allocation group. bootstrap sample excess share group calculated, average share translated allocation factor. smoother data-driven alternative using raw historical excess shares, especially large losses sparse still want allocation reflect observed group differences. "factor" Uses user supplied allocation_factor column. appropriate allocation follows pricing, underwriting governance decision rather observed large-loss split alone. example, factor can restrict allocation selected segments give one segment higher share based expert judgement. allocation arguments work together follows. allocation_by allocation_levels define part portfolio receives excess-loss component, example selected industry groups coverage segments. Rows outside levels receive allocation factor 0. Within selected part, allocation_weights defines volume measure used spread amount, usually exposure another earned-volume measure. allocation_factor row-level multiplier used allocation base: allocation_base = allocation_weights * allocation_factor. factor 0 means allocation, 1 means standard allocation, values 1 allocate relatively less excess-loss row group. allocation_method = "exposure", factor 1 selected rows. allocation_method = "historical_excess" "bootstrap_excess", factor derived final allocation follows observed bootstrapped excess shares group, allowing allocation_weights. allocation_method = "factor", factor read directly column named allocation_factor. output determines row-level vector returned directly. variants also stored attributes, add_excess_loss() can add several diagnostic columns without recalculating. fit_threshold excess_threshold different roles. fit_threshold determines claims used large-loss information. excess_threshold determines part added excess-loss. fit_threshold = 20000 excess_threshold = 100000, claims 20k 100k sampled bootstrap methods contribute zero excess pmax(sampled_amount - excess_threshold, 0).

title: Edit an existing smoothing step in a refinement workflow — edit_smoothing
code: set.seed(42) driver_age <- rep(seq(20, 59), each = 4) exposure <- rep(1, length(driver_age)) age_band <- cut(   driver_age,   breaks = c(18, 30, 40, 50, 60),   include.lowest = TRUE ) expected_claims <- exp(   -1.7 + 0.018 * (driver_age - 20) + 0.0006 * (driver_age - 40)^2 ) portfolio <- data.frame(   claims = rpois(length(driver_age), exposure * expected_claims),   exposure = exposure,   driver_age = driver_age,   age_band = age_band )  model <- glm(   claims ~ age_band + offset(log(exposure)),   family = poisson(),   data = portfolio )  refined <- prepare_refinement(model, data = portfolio) |>   add_smoothing(     model_variable = "age_band",     source_variable = "driver_age",     breaks = c(18, 30, 40, 50, 60),     degree = 2,     weights = "exposure"   ) |>   edit_smoothing(     model_variable = "age_band",     from = 30,     to = 50,     from_value = 1.00,     to_value = 1.10,     control_positions = c(40),     control_values = c(1.05)   )  refined_model <- refit(refined)

Missing levels automatically filled already fitted GLM relativities, users can adjust selected levels. add_relativities() now uses model_variable split_variable. relativities() replaces relativities_list() helper building relativity specifications. restrict_coef(), smooth_coef() refit_glm() remain deprecated compatibility wrappers now link clearly add_restriction(), add_smoothing() refit(). autoplot.rating_refinement() longer carries experimental badge uses package plot theme. refinement documentation expanded applied examples clearer explanation smoothing, restrictions, relativities refitting.
